content: I’m Sam, 31, backend/ML engineer at a Paris logistics startup. Date 2026-04-17. DECISION: DuckDB for the analysts' ad-hoc queries — cheap, simple, single-node. Current infra: Postgres for OLTP, S3 for raw Parquet, Airflow, Metabase for dashboards. Analysts are Leo, Maria, and Aisha (3 people). They run ~20 ad-hoc queries/day, concurrency usually 1–3. Typical dataset sizes they explore are 1–50 GB; occasional investigations might touch up to 200 GB compressed. Constraint: keep infra cost around €200/month and ops minimal. Rollout target: 2026-05-15. Fallback if limits hit: ClickHouse (single-node/cluster), or Snowflake if budget expands. I want reproducible local runs (notebooks), JDBC access from Metabase, nightly Parquet materialization via Airflow. Open questions: exact prod VM size, memory limits, monitoring, and a simple rollback plan. Thinking out loud to lock this in and record next steps.
Good choice for cheap/single-node. Quick question: do you expect any single interactive query to need to read more than ~200 GB compressed Parquet in production?

Sometimes, yes — once every few months we inspect big backups or long-range joins where raw input can be ~500 GB uncompressed, but compressed it’s usually <=200 GB. I can change analyst habits so typical interactive sessions stay <=100 GB compressed. Proposed prod VM: 8 vCPU, 64 GB RAM, 1 TB NVMe (estimate €180/month). Plan: Airflow DAG nightly writes partitioned Parquet to S3 + a nightly DuckDB DB file on the VM (or mount S3 via duckdb-httpfs), Metabase connects via DuckDB JDBC, analysts can also run queries locally against the same Parquet with DuckDB in notebooks. Rollout steps: provision VM, implement DAG by 2026-04-30, connect Metabase 2026-05-05, internal load-test with 3 analysts by 2026-05-10, go-live 2026-05-15. If concurrency or scan size becomes a problem, move to ClickHouse single-node first; Snowflake only if we accept >€1k/month. Need monitoring (memory, swap, CPU, query latency, GB scanned) and clear OOM/runaway query mitigation.
What’s your primary success metric at day 30 — e.g., 95th-percentile interactive query <10s or something else?

Day-30 success metrics: 95th-percentile for common ad-hoc queries <15 s, median <3 s. Cost <€200/month. Zero user-facing downtime during working hours. Monitoring in place for memory pressure, swap use, CPU, query time, and GB scanned per query. Team/ops: Claire (infra lead) owns primary on-call, I’m secondary. Weekly check-ins through 2026-06-01 to validate assumptions and the budget. If 95th exceeds 15 s or we see frequent OOMs, trigger contingency plan.
Who will be explicitly on-call for memory OOM incidents during business hours — Claire or you?

Claire primary weekdays 09:00–18:00 CET, I take evenings and weekends. Runbook: 1) identify and cancel runaway query in Metabase/SSH; 2) check duckdb process memory and free swap; 3) restart DuckDB service if needed; 4) if recurring, raise memory_limit to 48 GB and re-run problematic DAG; 5) if scans >200 GB for interactive use, pre-aggregate or move that workload to ClickHouse. Alerts go to #infra Slack via Prometheus/Alertmanager. Also set a query_timeout of 600 s for ad-hoc connections to prevent multi-hour scans.
Understood. One short question on data freshness: is 24-hour freshness acceptable for the analysts' ad-hoc use, or do you need sub-hour data for some use cases?

Mostly 24-hour is fine for analytics. For troubleshooting we keep last-15-minute data in Postgres materialized tables and a small real-time dashboard outside DuckDB. Communicate the scope to the team by 2026-05-10 so they don’t expect near-real-time in Metabase queries against DuckDB. That keeps the single-node DuckDB cheap and predictable.

Summary: DuckDB chosen; rollout by 2026-05-15; prod VM 8 vCPU/64 GB/1 TB NVMe (~€180/mo); Claire primary on-call (Sam secondary); day-30 success = 95th <15 s, median <3 s, cost <€200/mo; monitoring and runbook in place; fallback to ClickHouse or Snowflake if needs grow. Anything else to lock before you start provisioning?
